“A colossal failure at every level of government”
Nearly one year after the U.S. found its first known coronavirus case, the country is hurtling toward 400,000 total deaths, and cases, hospitalizations and deaths have reached record highs.

Interviews with more than 100 health, political and community leaders, along with a review of emails and other state government records offer a fuller picture of three major things that went wrong in the U.S.’s pandemic response.
1. The severity of the current outbreak can be traced to the rush to reopen last spring. Many governors moved quickly, sometimes acting over the objections of their advisers.
2. Science was sidelined at every level of government. More than 100 state and local health officials have been fired or have resigned since the beginning of the pandemic. https://nyti.ms/3iouCJb

3. Records show that at least 26 states ignored recommendations from the White House on masks and other health issues. Other states suffered, in part, from a lack of federal guidance. http://nyti.ms/3iouCJb

Shares of the struggling video game retailer GameStop surged on Monday, adding to a recent rally that has lifted the stock by more than 300% in January.

A swell of retail investors, many new to Wall Street investing, have been making a flurry of bets in the options market that stocks will go up. Some analysts say that the online frenzy could be fueling a stock market bubble.

Buying single-stock options has become especially popular among these online amateur investors who gather on Reddit’s “Wall Street Bets” and Discord to swap ideas and fawn over screenshots of both purported wins and gut-wrenching losses.

Even during the uncertainty the market faced at the start of the pandemic, investors — often with little experience — have been pouring into the market, lured by free trades, cash from relief checks or an itch for action with most sports leagues shut down. https://nyti.ms/3qS8pqg

The momentum will probably last until markets turn down and these newly minted traders experience painful losses that, for many, will be the first in what has been an extremely short investing career. If they flee, it will only magnify any fall. https://nyti.ms/3qS8pqg

The online trading app Robinhood promised to wrest the stock market away from Wall Street’s gatekeepers — making it as easy to put millions at risk as it is to summon an Uber.
It turns out, Robinhood was at the mercy of the industry it had vowed to upend.

This week, legions of armchair investors on Robinhood, who had been buying up options and shares of GameStop, caused a crisis on the market when they enlarged those bets and began making big trades in other stocks, including AMC Entertainment.
Robinhood limited trading in GameStop on Thursday, after the financial system’s risk reduction mechanisms kicked in, forcing the app to find emergency cash to continue to be able to trade.
On Thursday night, Robinhood took on an emergency infusion of more than $1 billion from investors. Furious users, meanwhile, flooded app stores with vitriolic reviews. https://nyti.ms/3oyhCCr

Robinhood’s distress follows a familiar narrative: A Silicon Valley company that promised to disrupt an industry ends up being overcome by the forces it unleashed and has to be reined in by regulators, or in this case, the industry it promised to change. https://nyti.ms/3oyhCCr

This week’s fiasco will almost certainly have consequences for the company. The SEC said it was looking into the events and lawmakers on both sides of the aisle called for hearings over complaints that customers were shut out of trades. https://nyti.ms/3oyhCCr
